event: Satan rallying the fallen angels after their expulsion from Heaven
symbols: Satan standing tall with raised weapon — defiant pride even after defeat, the arch-rebel who will not submit
prostrate fallen angels rising — the rallying of the demonic host for continued warfare against God
dark stormy sky — the spiritual darkness that enshrouds the kingdom of evil
barren landscape — the desolation of exile from God's presence
description: A dramatic black-and-white engraving depicting a large winged figure (Satan) standing atop a hill or mound, holding a spear or staff aloft. He has bat-like wings and a commanding posture. Below and around him, numerous fallen angels in various states of defeat and recovery sprawl across the barren landscape. Some are rising, others still prostrate. The sky is dark and turbulent with dramatic cloud formations. The scene conveys both defeat and defiant resurgence.
theological_description: This engraving from Milton's Paradise Lost depicts Satan rallying his defeated host after their expulsion from Heaven, but it draws on deep biblical imagery. The scene evokes Isaiah 14:12-15: 'How you are fallen from heaven, O Day Star! You said in your heart, I will ascend above the heights of the clouds; I will make myself like the Most High. But you are brought down to Sheol.' Despite this catastrophic defeat, Satan's defiance persists — 'Better to reign in Hell than serve in Heaven,' as Milton wrote, capturing the essence of pride that refuses to repent. Revelation 12:7-9 describes the cosmic war: 'Michael and his angels fought against the dragon... and the great dragon was thrown down, that ancient serpent, who is called the devil and Satan.' The fallen angels around Satan are the 'angels who did not stay within their own position of authority' (Jude 1:6). Yet Scripture assures believers that this rebellion is already defeated: Christ 'disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in the cross' (Col 2:15).

scripture_refs.reason: The fall of the Day Star from heaven, the pride that drives Satan's continued rebellion
The war in heaven and Satan cast down with his angels — the event behind this scene
Angels who did not keep their proper position — the fallen host rallied by Satan
Christ disarming the principalities — the ultimate defeat of these defiant forces
God did not spare angels when they sinned but cast them into chains of darkness

_meta.title: They heard, and were abashed, and up they sprung
